topic: A person discovers the tree they planted as a child has been cut down
raw_output:
[story 1]

The sudden emptiness where the oak had stood was a physical blow. Elias stood at the perimeter of the clearing, his hands clenched so tightly his knuckles were white. He stared at the raw, splintered stump, a jagged scar against the earth where his childhood sanctuary used to be. The air smelled wrong—a sharp, metallic tang mingling with the damp rot of freshly disturbed soil. He took a slow, shallow breath, the movement catching painfully in his chest. "No," he whispered, the sound brittle and thin. He knelt down, brushing away the loose debris, his fingers tracing the unnatural, clean cut on the trunk. A wave of profound nausea washed over him, making his stomach churn violently. He felt a hot flush creep up his neck, and he couldn't look at the remaining stumps; they seemed to mock him with their brutal finality.

[story 2]

"This isn't right," Sarah muttered, her voice tight, as she surveyed the devastation. She walked around the perimeter of the plot, her steps heavy and deliberate, each footfall sounding like a complaint against the silence. The space where the towering maple had been was now just an uneven patch of mud and broken branches. She stopped by the base of the remaining wood, bringing a hand up to touch the rough bark. The texture felt alien, wrong, like touching something spoiled. Her gaze swept over the discarded remnants—shredded leaves, snapped limbs—and a strange, cold tightening seized her throat. She turned away abruptly, walking toward the edge of the property line, needing distance from the scene, her jaw set so hard it ached.

[story 3]

I found it by accident while trimming the back hedge. It was the oak. The one I’d painstakingly nurtured from a tiny sapling when I was seven. Now, it was just a pile of expensive, pale wood, carelessly stacked. My breath hitched, shallow and quick. I dropped the shears; the clatter sounded deafening in the quiet afternoon. I walked toward the wreckage, my gait stiff, my eyes refusing to settle on the sight. The sheer waste of effort, the careless severing of something so rooted, made my skin crawl. I bent down, picking up a shard of bark, and the texture felt abrasive, repellent beneath my fingertips. I stood up slowly, shaking my head, unable to reconcile what I saw with what I remembered.
